title: La Vuelta Femenina 2026 preview: Route, favourites, how to watch and start list
content:
Cyclist
La Vuelta Femenina 2026 preview: Route, favourites, how to watch and start list

After the rush of the Classics, stage racing returns. The Vuelta Femenina 2026 is the first women’s Grand Tour of the season, beginning on Sunday 3rd May and running over seven stages, ending on what should be a dramatic Angliru finish on 9th May.

Starting in Marín on Sunday and taking place entirely in the confines of northwestern Spain, the Vuelta doesn’t include any time-trials but packs in two Asturias summit finishes of Les Praeres and Alto de l’Angliru for a final weekend of GC action at this year’s edition.

Last year, it was Demi Vollering who delivered another masterclass in the race, winning two stages and the red jersey for her second consecutive Vuelta title. Behind her, the fight for second was closely fought, with Marlen Reusser and Anna van der Breggen filling the lower steps of the podium. Vollering won’t be there this year to defend her title, instead opting to ride the Giro d’Italia, with Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney, Van der Breggen, Sarah Gigante and Pauline Ferrand-Prévot among those looking to take her crown.

La Vuelta Femenina 2026 key information

  • Date: Sunday 3rd May – Saturday 9th May 2026
  • Start: Marín
  • Finish: Alto de l’Angliru
  • Total distance: 815km
  • Total elevation: 14,486m
  • Live TV coverage: HBO Max, TNT Sports, Peacock, SBS, FloBikes
  • Most wins: Annemiek van Vleuten (three)
  • 2025 winner: Demi Vollering (FDJ-Suez)

La Vuelta Femenina 2026 route

The 2026 Vuelta Femenina is seven stages in length as it was last year, still one shorter than its peak of eight in 2024. While the past two years have commenced with a team time-trial, 2026 sees a punchy uphill finish serving up the first red jersey of the race.

From there, it’s really one for the puncheurs, breakaway artists and pure climbers as the route winds through plenty of hilly stages before back-to-back summit finishes to crown the winner of this year’s edition.

La Vuelta Femenina 2026 stage-by-stage preview

Stage 1: Sunday 3rd May, Marín – Salvaterra de Miño, 113km

The opening stage of the Vuelta Femenina has typically been a team time-trial, but there will be no race against the clock in this edition. Instead, the peloton departs Marín over a couple of classified climbs that won’t bring too much stress. There’s still some more bumps to contend with before the punchy uphill finish in Salvaterra de Miño.

Stage 2: Monday 4th May, Lobios – San Cibrao das Viñas, 109km

The second stage doesn’t feature any categorised climbs, but it’s not a flat day by any means. There’s an uncategorised climb just prior to the finish line, before the last few hundred metres head downhill. This looks to be one for either a breakaway or a reduced bunch sprint.

Stage 3: Tuesday 5th May, Padrón – A Coruña, 121km

You’re getting the picture by now. No categorised climbs on the menu, but it’s still an undulating 121km route from Padrón to A Coruña, with the route continuing to stick to the confines of the Galicia region so far.

Stage 4: Wednesday 6th May, Monforte de Lemos – Antas de Ulla, 115km

Stage 4 brings two Category 3 climbs and another slight uphill to the line for Galicia’s final offering at the 2026 La Vuelta Femenina.

Stage 5: Thursday 7th May, León – Astorga, 119km

Another couple of Category 3 climbs await the peloton for what looks like a calm stage before the mountains to come.

Stage 6: Friday 8th May, Gijón/Xixón – Les Praeres, Nava, 106km

Stage 6 brings the first summit finish of the race at Les Praeres. The final climb is only 3.9km but reaches a maximum gradient of 23% after 1.5km of climbing, with the overall average gradient coming to 13%.

Stage 7: Sunday 9th May, La Pola Llaviana/Pola de Lavianna – L’Angliru, 132km

Stage 7 is the big one. Not only is it the longest stage of the race, it also features four climbs including the main event: the Alto de l’Angliru. Organisers initially weren’t convinced about including the mythically difficult Asturian climb in the race’s parcours, but after consulting riders and teams, gave it the green light.

The Angliru is 13km long with a 9.7% average, but its second half is utterly savage, regularly breaching 20%. This difficult a climb is a rarity in women’s cycling, and should serve to makes this race highly unpredictable.

La Vuelta Femenina 2026 favourites

With no Demi Vollering at this year’s race, it is a massive opportunity for someone else to take the red jersey. It’s an interesting route with no multi-mountain stages, just two summit finishes including the monstrous Angliru.

While the sensible choices to take the victory would be Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney and Anna van der Breggen, who showed some impressive form in the Ardennes Classics, this route arguably suits climber Sarah Gigante more than anyone, with the steep Angliru slopes and lack of mountain descents ideal for her superior power-to-weight talent.

Pauline Ferrand-Prévot is a cautious favourite – while she has been strong in the Classics, we saw in 2025 that she only found her stage race form at the Tour de France after almost three months out post-Vuelta. She could well prove to be an outlier next week, but considering she was only able to manage 22nd at Liège last weekend it seems unlikely she’ll come out on top in Asturias.

There are also a few teams yet to announce their squads for this one, but we’ll endeavour to update our ratings when they get confirmed.

Cyclist’s ratings

★★★★★: Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney (Canyon-SRAM-Zondacrypto), Anna van der Breggen (SD Worx-Protime)
★★★★: Sarah Gigante (AG Insurance-Soudal), Pauline Ferrand-Prévot (Visma-Lease a Bike)
★★★: Juliette Berthet (FDJ United-Suez), Monica Trinca Colonel (Liv AlUla Jayco)
★★: Usoa Ostolaza (Laboral Kutxa-Fundacion Euskadi), Valentina Cavallar (SD Worx-Protime), Evita Muzic (FDJ United-Suez)
★: Femke de Vries (Visma-Lease a Bike), Urska Zigart (AG Insurance-Soudal), Mavi Garcia (UAE Team ADQ), Paula Blasi (UAE Team ADQ)

How to watch La Vuelta Femenina 2026

La Vuelta Femenina will be broadcast live on TNT Sports 1 throughout the week in the UK and Ireland. For coverage without ad breaks (touch wood), tune in through HBO Max. Elsewhere, fans in the US can watch the racing on Peacock, Canada will be able to stream the action on FloBikes, it’ll be on SBS in Australia, and Sky Sport in New Zealand.

If the race isn’t being broadcast in your country or you are travelling abroad, a VPN will allow you to hide your device’s location to access content that is normally geo-blocked.
